title: Analog quantum simulation of chiral magnetic dynamics using optical superlattices
abstract: We propose an analog quantum simulation of chiral magnetic dynamics using ultracold atoms in an optical superlattice. The massive Schwinger model in the zero gauge coupling limit maps onto the Rice-Mele model, with the fermion mass and topological angle encoded in the superlattice parameters. We study the real-time dynamics of the vector current following two quench protocols that drive continuous chirality injection and chirality relaxation. Simulations with realistic superlattice parameters and experimental noise demonstrates clear mass dependence of the current dynamics in both protocols, robust against experimental imperfections. The vector current may be directly measurable via single-bond-resolved detection, establishing cold atom superlattices as a viable platform for probing non-equilibrium chiral phenomena.
summary: The authors propose using ultracold atoms in engineered optical superlattices to analogously simulate chiral magnetic dynamics governed by the massive Schwinger model. By implementing controlled quenches of topological angle or chemical potential, they measure the resulting vector current, confirming that this platform is robust and capable of probing complex non-equilibrium quantum phenomena.
main_problem: The paper addresses the analog quantum simulation of chiral magnetic dynamics, which is related to the Chiral Magnetic Effect (CME).
main_result: They demonstrate that ultracold atoms in optical superlattices provide a viable platform for probing non-equilibrium chiral phenomena, showing clear mass dependence in current dynamics.
method: The study uses two quench protocols—a topological angle quench and a chiral chemical potential quench—to drive the system's evolution.
model_or_system: The physical platform is ultracold atoms trapped in an optical superlattice. The underlying model is derived from the massive Schwinger model, which maps onto the Rice-Mele tight-binding model.
key_observables: The spatially averaged vector current (J), which serves as the analogue for the CME current.
important_parameters: Mass ($m$), topological angle ($	heta$), and superlattice parameters like $V_\ell$ and $\varphi$.
main_assumptions: The mapping to the Rice-Mele model requires approximations, particularly when simulating the continuous chiral chemical potential quench ($\mu_5$).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows how mass and topological angle are controlled by superlattice depths/phases. Figure 2 illustrates current dynamics during a $	heta$ quench, while Figure 3 shows evolution during a $\mu_5$ quench.
paper_structure: The paper first derives the lattice Hamiltonian from the continuum Schwinger model (using staggered fermions). It then maps this to the Rice-Mele model and simulates its real-time dynamics using two distinct quench protocols, analyzing the resulting vector current.
why_interesting: This work directly bridges advanced condensed matter theory (CME, massive Schwinger model) with cutting-edge experimental quantum simulation techniques using cold atoms in optical lattices, making it highly relevant for AMO physicists studying non-equilibrium many-body physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Realized mass m (a) and topological angle θ (b) as a function of the relative phase φ and depth Vℓ of the secondary lattice, at fixed primary-lattice depth Vs = 8ER, with ER the recoil energy. White curves are contours of constant m/w and θ/π. Dashed lines trace the curves Vℓ(φ) that correspond to a fixed m/w = 0.15 (blue) and m/w = 0.30 (red), while θ is varied. In (a) the colormap saturates for m/w > 1, since that region lies outside the light-mass regime of interest.

Figure 2: Vector current ¯J following a θ quench from θ = 0 to θf. The time evolution is computed using the Rice-Mele Hamiltonian on an open chain of N = 30 sites, and ¯J is determined using Eq. (9). Panels show (a) m/w = 0.15 and (b) m/w = 0.30, with θf = 0.2 (blue), 0.4 (red), and 0.6 (green). Solid lines are the central (unperturbed) configuration, whereas shaded regions are bands obtained from systematic perturbations of the superlattice parameters Vs (±2.5%), Vℓ(±2.5%), and φ (±0.02 rad). The dashed region is given by the maximal and minimal value obtained within those error windows.

Figure 3: Vector current ¯J following a µ5 quench, θ(t) = −2µ5t. The evolution is computed using the Rice-Mele Hamiltonian on an open chain of N = 30 sites. Panels show (a) m/w = 0.15 and (b) m/w = 0.30, with µ5/w = 0.1 (blue), 0.2 (red), and 0.3 (green). Solid lines and shaded regions are defined as in Fig. 2.

Figure 2 shows for m/w = 0.15 and m/w = 0.30, the current ¯J after a sudden change of θ from 0 to θf. We recall that for the θ quench the mapping to the Rice- Mele model is exact, and hence the quantum simulation of model (3) is in principle exact. However, experimen- tally, there may be systematic errors in the superlattice parameters. The shaded bands in Figs. 2 are obtained by considering ±2.5% deviations in the value of Vs and Vℓ, and a deviation of ±0.02 rad in the value of φ. The sys- tematic errors introduce a non-negligible effect at longer times, especially for larger masses and larger θf values, due to the stiff dependence of θ on φ, see Fig. 1 (b). Figure 4: Validity of the superlattice-based quantum sim- ulation for the µ5 quench for (a) m/w = 0.7 and (b) m/w = 0.9. The vector current ¯J/w evaluated using the Rice-Mele model (dashed curves) is compared with the exact dynamics given by model (3) (solid curves), for µ5 = 0.2w (blue) and µ5 = 0.5w (red).
